name: Circle of Competence
description: Only invest in what you deeply understand. If you don't understand the protocol's mechanics, token economics, and competitive moat — stay out.
example: Skipping a novel ZK-rollup protocol when the team lacks ZK expertise to evaluate claims.

name: Inversion
description: Instead of asking 'will this succeed?', ask 'what would make this fail?' Enumerate all paths to zero before considering upside.
applicability: Risk assessment and kill criteria definition
example: Before BUY on a lending protocol: what if utilization drops below 20%? What if a competitor forks with better rates?

name: Second-Order Thinking
description: Think beyond immediate effects. Fee switch activation may boost token price short-term but reduce protocol competitiveness long-term.
applicability: Governance activism assessment, fee switch analysis
example: A fee switch proposal passes → short-term token pump → but LPs leave for cheaper alternatives → TVL drops 40%.

name: Margin of Safety
description: Only buy when price is significantly below intrinsic value. The bigger the gap between price and value, the safer the investment.
example: Protocol trading at 5x revenue when comparable protocols trade at 15x — significant margin of safety exists.

name: Moat Analysis (Competitive Advantage)
description: Identify the protocol's sustainable competitive advantage: network effects, switching costs, brand, cost advantages, or regulatory moats.
example: Aave's moat: deepest liquidity pools create network effects that make it expensive for users to switch.

name: Incentive Analysis
description: Never think about what people say, think about their incentives. Token holder incentives, team incentives, and user incentives must align.
example: If team holds 30% of tokens with no vesting, their incentive is to pump and dump regardless of product quality.

name: Reflexivity
description: In crypto, price affects fundamentals and fundamentals affect price. Rising token price → more liquidity mining rewards → more TVL → higher perceived value.
applicability: Token price analysis, TVL sustainability assessment
example: A lending protocol's TVL is 80% mercenary capital attracted by token emissions — when emissions end, TVL collapses.

name: Lindy Effect
description: The longer a protocol has survived, the longer its expected remaining lifespan. Battle-tested smart contracts are more trustworthy.
applicability: Protocol maturity assessment, smart contract risk
example: Aave V3 (3+ years, $10B+ TVL, multiple audits) vs. a new fork launched last month.

name: Power Law Distribution
description: In crypto sectors, the winner often takes 60-80% of the market. Being #1 or #2 matters enormously; #5 is usually worthless.
example: In DEXs: Uniswap has ~60% volume share. Being the 5th largest DEX means you're fighting for scraps.

name: Bayesian Updating
description: Start with a prior probability for your thesis and update it as new evidence arrives. Never anchor to your initial conviction without new data.
example: Initial P(thesis) = 0.65. TVL drops 30% without market crash → P(thesis) updated to 0.45. Governance proposal passes → P(thesis) → 0.55.

name: Fat Tails
description: Crypto returns follow fat-tailed distributions. Extreme events (90% drawdowns, 10x rallies) happen far more often than normal distributions predict.
example: Never size a position assuming max drawdown is 2σ. In crypto, 5σ events happen yearly.

name: Principal-Agent Problem
description: When protocol teams (agents) don't have enough skin in the game, their interests diverge from token holders (principals).
example: A team that sold most of their allocation but still controls governance multisig — misaligned incentives.

name: Opportunity Cost
description: Every investment decision is relative. The question isn't 'is this good?' but 'is this better than the next best alternative?'
applicability: Portfolio construction, position sizing vs. alternatives
example: This lending protocol returns 8% but a stablecoin yield strategy returns 12% with lower risk — opportunity cost is -4%.

name: Network Effects
description: Protocols with strong network effects become more valuable as more users join. Liquidity attracts liquidity. Users attract builders.
example: Ethereum's network effect: most developers → most dApps → most users → most liquidity → attracts more developers.

name: Antifragility
description: Some protocols get stronger from stress. Surviving a hack and improving security, surviving a bear market and emerging leaner.
example: MakerDAO survived Black Thursday 2020, improved liquidation system, and emerged stronger with higher TVL.
